chunks.text: My family raises eight - year - old twin daughters, Emma and Sophia.
Their similarity is surprisingly strong, but their personalities are characteristically different.
Emma is logical and thinks about things in an orderly way.
She is good at math and science
and she often devises puzzles to entertain the family.
Sophia, on the other hand, is sensitive and artistic.
She draws, sings, and reads out the stories she has made up to the family.
Even adults can be astonish at her power of representation.
Genetically, identical twins should be the same, yet as they grew
each one's personality has become more and more distinct.
This provides an interesting reference for studies of " nature versus nurture.
Genes alone do not determine personality.
Environment, experience, and the person's own choices shape each one.
If even twins differ, of course every human is unique.
What matters in raising a child is respecting each one's personality.
Making Emma draw the same picture as Sophia is pointless.
Making Sophia solve the same puzzle as Emma is painful.
The parents' role is to cultivate each one's strength.
But parents are not the only ones who raise excellent human beings.
School teachers, friends, local community.
And the child's own effort.
Altogether, they shape a single person.
Emma and Sophia are growing up today, quarreling and laughing.
As they grow up, what kind of adults will they become?
As a parent, I am fairly looking forward to it.
